title: Method vs Function — this Binding
explanation: obj.greet() — 'this' is implicitly bound to obj. But greet = obj.greet loses the binding. Standalone call → this is undefined (strict) or global (sloppy).
mentalModel: this depends on HOW a function is called, not WHERE it's defined

title: Arrow Function vs Regular Function this
explanation: diameter() is a regular function → this = shape. perimeter is an arrow → this = enclosing scope (global), where this.radius is undefined. undefined * number = NaN.
mentalModel: Arrow functions don't have their own 'this' — they inherit from enclosing scope at definition time

title: call() Executes Immediately, bind() Returns Copy
explanation: .call() invokes immediately with this=person. .bind() returns a NEW function with bound this — it does NOT invoke. The second log shows the function itself.
mentalModel: call/apply = invoke NOW with this. bind = create new function with permanent this (invoke later)

title: Without new — Constructor Pollutes Global
explanation: Without 'new', this refers to the global object. We accidentally set global.firstName and global.lastName. The function returns nothing → sarah = undefined.
mentalModel: Without 'new', constructor functions return undefined and pollute the global scope

title: new + Explicit Object Return — Override
explanation: When a constructor explicitly returns an OBJECT, that overrides the default 'this'. If it returns a primitive, the return is ignored and 'this' is used.
mentalModel: Constructor + object return = returned object wins. Primitive return = ignored

title: this in setTimeout with Method Call
explanation: Both calls invoke getStatus as data.getStatus(), so 'this' is implicitly bound to data in both cases. The arrow function in setTimeout doesn't affect the inner method's this.
mentalModel: What matters is the call-site: obj.method() always binds this to obj

title: Arrow Function in Object Method — updateEmail
explanation: updateEmail is an arrow function — it doesn't bind its own 'this'. 'this' refers to the global scope, not the user object. So this.email = 'new@email.com' sets a property on the global object, not on user.
mentalModel: Never use arrow functions as object methods — they can't access the object via 'this'

title: Static Methods Can't Be Called on Instances
output: TypeError: freddie.colorChange is not a function
explanation: Static methods live on the constructor, not on instances. freddie is an instance of Chameleon, so colorChange doesn't exist on it. Call it as Chameleon.colorChange('orange') instead.
mentalModel: Static methods belong to the class itself, not to instances. Instances can't access them

title: typeof IIFE Return Value
explanation: sayHi returns the result of an IIFE that returns 0. typeof 0 = 'number'. The confusing part is thinking the IIFE returns a function — it doesn't, it's immediately invoked and returns 0.
mentalModel: IIFE = immediately invoked. The return value is the result of execution, not the function itself
